title: Vendrix Platform Engineering
tagline: Managed v2 frontend and backend delivery, ERP integrations, and cloud platform operations for a construction finance product later acquired by Foundation Software.
summary: At Vendrix, I managed one engineer while leading frontend and backend delivery for the v2 release, ERP and API-partner integrations, and the GCP platform work behind secure, repeatable operations.
problem: Vendrix needed a stronger v2 product release for construction finance workflows, with reliable integrations, a flexible transaction model, secure cloud operations, and delivery leadership that could move product and platform work together.

visual.alt: Vendrix booth at Concrete Expo in Las Vegas
visual.title: Concrete Expo, Las Vegas
visual.caption: Vendrix represented in front of builders and finance stakeholders at Concrete Expo in Las Vegas.

approach: Manage one engineer while owning the v2 frontend and backend release plan, coordinating product delivery across application work, integrations, and platform concerns.
Design API-partner and third-party ERP integrations around robust schemas, including a transaction-tagging model that linked spend to budgets, projects, resources, tools, and equipment.
Architect and operate GCP infrastructure across Cloud Run, Cloud Functions, Pub/Sub, Cloud Tasks, Scheduler, and storage-triggered workflows while improving CI/CD, migration paths, and observability.

architecture.runtime: Frontend and backend services supported transaction capture, enrichment, and downstream workflow execution across partner and ERP-connected flows.
architecture.deployment: Cloud Build and Artifact Registry provided repeatable, auditable deployments while migration work improved the path from development to production.
architecture.data: A tagging-oriented transaction schema let customers associate activity with budgets, jobs, resources, and equipment while keeping integration data coherent across systems.
architecture.observability: Cloud Logging and Cloud Monitoring were wired into the platform so incidents, regressions, and operational risk surfaced quickly.

outcomes: Delivered the v2 release while managing both application delivery and the supporting platform work.
Shipped a flexible transaction-tagging model that gave customers stronger control over how spend connected to budgets, projects, resources, and equipment.
Supported the product and software-operations maturity of Vendrix before its acquisition by Foundation Software.
notes: My role covered management, product delivery, platform engineering, and software operations rather than the acquisition transaction itself.
The acquisition context is based on the public Foundation Software and Thoma Bravo announcement linked with this project.

title: EdgeWatch Telemetry
tagline: Offline-first ops capture and edge telemetry with heartbeat monitoring and alert routing.
summary: An operations-focused system pairing offline-capable workflows with buffered edge telemetry, health checks, and actionable alerts.
problem: Operational data was scattered across photos, notes, and manual checks while connectivity remained unreliable. Critical well status depended on humans noticing problems before the system could.
approach: Use offline-first capture with local queues, background sync, and deterministic retries so field workflows stay usable under poor connectivity.
Buffer edge telemetry and sign heartbeat messages so health status remains explicit even when devices reconnect later.
Route alerts with dedupe, throttling, and quiet-hours controls so operators see actionable signals instead of noise.

architecture.runtime: An offline-first PWA handles field capture while a Raspberry Pi agent buffers and forwards telemetry to cloud-side processing and alerting.
architecture.deployment: Cloud-side services stay lightweight and event-driven so storage, indexing, and alert workflows remain easy to operate.
architecture.data: Operational records, media, and telemetry events are stored with immutable identifiers and idempotent processing to prevent duplicate handling.
architecture.observability: Heartbeat health, device state, and alert-routing behavior are surfaced through dashboards and notifications.

outcomes: Reduced manual checks by roughly 60 to 80 percent in agriculture operations work.
Improved outage detection from roughly 6 to 12 hours to under 10 minutes.
Showcases reliability patterns for distributed operations where connectivity and field conditions are part of the system design.
notes: Built to keep ops overhead low while still giving teams searchable records, telemetry visibility, and escalation paths.
Public summary reflects real agriculture-operations delivery patterns.

title: EventPulse Data Platform
tagline: Contracts, schema-drift handling, lineage, and replayable warehouse delivery on GCP.
summary: A governed data-platform implementation shaped by client work: event-driven ingestion, data contracts, lineage artifacts, curated models, and published data products.
problem: Periodic file feeds arrived with changing schemas, limited lineage, and brittle downstream reporting. The goal was to turn manual, error-prone ingestion into a governed and replayable platform.
approach: Define data contracts for incoming feeds, then classify schema drift as additive or breaking before promoting data.
Capture lineage from raw source through contract version and curated outputs so replay, backfill, and audit workflows stay explicit.
Model warehouse layers in a raw-to-curated-to-marts pattern and publish governed views or APIs from validated outputs.

architecture.runtime: Event-driven processors validate inbound files, persist immutable raw inputs, and load curated warehouse tables and published views.
architecture.deployment: GCP services are provisioned with Terraform and deployed through CI/CD gates with environment parity and operational runbooks.
architecture.data: Contracts, validation thresholds, replay and backfill workflows, and lineage artifacts make upstream change visible instead of implicit.
architecture.observability: Freshness, failures, and anomaly checks feed dashboards and alerts so data-product breakage is caught early.

outcomes: Represents the same architecture patterns used in real client delivery for schema drift, replay and backfill, and governed publishing.
Creates a reusable foundation for BigQuery analytics, APIs, and downstream decision support.
Shows how data contracts and lineage become operational controls rather than documentation only.
notes: NDA-safe summary based on real client platform work.
Designed to make reprocessing, drift response, and published data products explicit.

title: Grounded Knowledge Platform
tagline: Citation-backed retrieval with refusal mode, evaluation gates, and confidentiality-aware deployment patterns.
summary: A grounded AI knowledge system focused on content architecture, metadata strategy, operational safety, and evidence-backed answers.
problem: High-value operational and policy knowledge lived in documents, making lookup slow and answer quality inconsistent. The system needed to speed retrieval without sacrificing confidentiality or traceability.
approach: Ingest and enrich documents with metadata, classification tags, and retrieval-friendly chunking before indexing.
Require citations and refusal behavior so the system returns grounded answers or clearly declines when evidence is insufficient.
Tie retrieval quality and safety to an evaluation harness so changes are regression-tested over time.

architecture.runtime: Containerized services handle ingestion, indexing, retrieval, and answer generation with deployment posture matched to data sensitivity.
architecture.deployment: Public-safe environments use Cloud Run and Terraform patterns; confidentiality-first variants can stay inside a controlled boundary.
architecture.data: Document metadata, classification tags, retention signals, and citation links are treated as first-class parts of the system.
architecture.observability: Audit logs, evaluation runs, answer latency, and refusal behavior provide operational feedback instead of blind prompting.

outcomes: Reduced document lookup from roughly 5 to 10 minutes to under 1 minute in applied client work.
Improved ownership-transition readiness with source-cited answers instead of undocumented tribal knowledge.
Demonstrates secure delivery patterns that span public-safe demos and confidentiality-first deployments.
notes: Built around citation-required retrieval, refusal mode, and evaluation regression rather than chatbot-style free-form generation.
Summarized in public-safe terms while reflecting real consulting delivery patterns.
